evals.name: new-rest-api-load-test
evals.prompt: I need to write a Locust load test for our e-commerce API in Python. The flow is: POST /api/auth/login to get a JWT token, then GET /api/products (browsed 3× more than checkout), then POST /api/orders for checkout. We expect 100 concurrent users, ramp up over 20 seconds, run for 10 minutes. SLA is p95 under 1000ms. I have a CSV file with test users.
evals.expected_output: A complete locustfile with: HttpUser class, on_start doing login and storing token as self.token, _auth_headers() helper, @task(3) for products and @task(1) for checkout, catch_response=True on all requests with response.failure() calls, between(1,3) wait_time, name= grouping on dynamic URLs, env vars for host/credentials, and the exact locust CLI command with --headless -u 100 -r 5 -t 10m --host.

assertions.name: uses_4_block_pattern
assertions.description: Locustfile has imports/config block, HttpUser class with on_start and tasks, optional events block, and run command comment

assertions.name: per_user_auth_in_on_start
assertions.description: Each virtual user authenticates in on_start() storing token as self.token — not a shared global token

assertions.name: uses_catch_response
assertions.description: All task requests use catch_response=True with explicit response.failure() calls for non-200 responses

assertions.name: task_weights_reflect_distribution
assertions.description: Products task has higher weight (3) than checkout task (1) matching the 3× browsing ratio

assertions.name: has_meaningful_wait_time
assertions.description: wait_time uses between() or constant_throughput() — not constant(0) or missing

assertions.name: uses_env_vars_for_credentials
assertions.description: Host, username, and password come from os.environ — not hardcoded strings

assertions.name: includes_run_command
assertions.description: Response includes the locust CLI command with --headless -u 100 -r 5 -t 10m --host flags

evals.name: fix-silent-failures
evals.expected_output: Explains that Locust marks a request as failed only when there is a connection error or timeout — HTTP 4xx/5xx responses still count as 'successful' requests in stats unless catch_response=True is used with response.failure(). Delivers fixed task using catch_response=True context manager with status check and body validation.

assertions.name: identifies_root_cause
assertions.description: Explains that without catch_response=True, Locust counts any completed HTTP response (including 4xx/5xx) as a success in stats

assertions.name: adds_catch_response
assertions.description: Fixed task uses 'with self.client.get(..., catch_response=True) as response:' pattern

assertions.name: adds_response_failure_call
assertions.description: Fixed task calls response.failure() with a descriptive message when status is unexpected

assertions.name: adds_body_validation
assertions.description: Fixed task also validates response body content — not just status code

evals.name: spike-test-load-shape
evals.prompt: We're running a flash sale next week. Normal traffic is 20 concurrent users but we expect a sudden burst to 500 users within 30 seconds when the sale starts. After 5 minutes at peak we expect it to drop back to 20. How do I model this in Locust?
evals.expected_output: Complete locustfile with LoadTestShape subclass implementing the spike pattern: baseline 20 users → spike to 500 with high spawn_rate (50+) → hold 5 minutes → ramp down to 20 → end. Explains that -u and -r flags must NOT be passed when using LoadTestShape. Includes run command without -u/-r.

assertions.name: uses_load_test_shape
assertions.description: Solution uses a LoadTestShape subclass with a tick() method — not just -u and -r flags

assertions.name: spike_to_500_users
assertions.description: Shape reaches 500 users during the peak stage

assertions.name: high_spawn_rate_for_spike
assertions.description: Spawn rate during the spike stage is high (≥ 50 users/sec) to simulate a sudden burst

assertions.name: holds_peak_for_5_minutes
assertions.description: Shape maintains 500 users for approximately 5 minutes (300 seconds)

assertions.name: run_command_omits_u_and_r
assertions.description: The locust run command does NOT include -u or -r flags since LoadTestShape controls user count

evals.name: fix-exploding-stats-table
evals.prompt: My Locust report has thousands of rows like /product/1, /product/2, /product/3... and I can't read anything. How do I fix this?
evals.expected_output: Identifies root cause: dynamic URL paths create a separate stats row per unique URL. Fix is to add name='/product/[id]' parameter to group them under one stats entry. Shows before/after code example. Mentions this applies to any parameterized URL.

assertions.name: identifies_root_cause
assertions.description: Explains that each unique URL path creates a separate row in Locust's stats table

assertions.name: uses_name_parameter
assertions.description: Fix adds name='/product/[id]' (or similar grouping pattern) to the client call

assertions.name: shows_before_after
assertions.description: Response includes both the wrong code (without name=) and the corrected code (with name=)

assertions.name: generalizes_the_pattern
assertions.description: Explains this applies to any parameterized URL — not just /product/[id]

evals.name: csv-data-parameterization
evals.prompt: I need each virtual user in my Locust test to log in with different credentials from a CSV file called users.csv with columns username,password. I have 500 users in the file and expect to run 100 VUs. How do I load and distribute the CSV data without all users sharing the same row?
evals.expected_output: Complete solution loading CSV once at module level (not inside the class or on_start) using csv module or pandas. Uses an iterator or index based on user_id or a queue to distribute rows across VUs. Stores per-user credentials as self.username/self.password in on_start. Handles the case where VU count exceeds CSV rows (cycling).

assertions.name: loads_csv_at_module_level
assertions.description: CSV is loaded once at module level (outside the class) — not reloaded in on_start or in each task

assertions.name: distributes_rows_across_vus
assertions.description: Each VU gets a different row using user_id index, modulo cycling, or a queue — not all VUs using row 0

assertions.name: stores_credentials_as_instance_attrs
assertions.description: Credentials are stored as self.username and self.password in on_start — not as globals

assertions.name: handles_vu_count_exceeds_csv
assertions.description: Solution handles the case where number of VUs exceeds CSV rows (e.g., using modulo: users[self.user_id % len(users)])
